main_dirt.bias_notes: Deep, sandy surface that becomes highly tiring late in the summer meet. Early speed dominates short sprints (under 6.5F), but the deep surface chews up one-dimensional speed in routes.
main_dirt.post_bias: Inside posts are a massive advantage at 1 1/8 miles because the starting gate is located immediately before the first turn.

mellon_turf_outer.bias_notes: Wider sweeping turns. Tactical speed or mid-pack stalkers tend to excel. Early speed generally struggles to go wire-to-wire here.
mellon_turf_outer.post_bias: Outside posts (7 and higher) historically perform disproportionately well on this outer course compared to the inner turf.

inner_turf.bias_notes: Features very tight, sharp turns. Visually favors smaller, athletic horses that can corner well over large, long-striding bulky horses.
inner_turf.post_bias: The inside three posts offer a significant advantage, especially at 1 mile and 1 1/16 miles.

handicapping_angles.the_belmont_flip: Downgrade horses whose best speed figures came in 1-turn routes at Belmont. Upgrade true 2-turn route specialists coming from tracks like Churchill, Aqueduct, or past Saratoga meets.
handicapping_angles.late_meet_fade: As the meet progresses into August, the dirt rail often becomes 'dead' and cuppy, shifting the route bias heavily toward closers.
handicapping_angles.juvenile_sprints: In 2-year-old dirt sprints under 6 furlongs, early speed on the pace or within a length of the lead wins at a nearly 50% rate.
handicapping_angles.turf_trip_quality: On the grass, trip quality (saving ground, avoiding traffic) dictates winners more than raw speed figures. Wide-drawn closers in big fields are highly vulnerable.
